text: Il Servizio di Chirurgia Generale dell'Ospedale Universitario Marquès de Val d'Isilla presents robotic sigmadectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis using the Wige and Pop technique.
Il Servizio di Chirurgia Generale dell'Ospedale Universitario Marquès de Val d'Isilla presents robotic sigmadectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis using the Wige and Pop technique.

The majority of left-sided robotic colorectal procedures are associated to a laparotomy before the anastomosis fashioning. This lengthens the procedure, obliging the surgeon to leave his robotic console.

The majority of left-sided robotic colorectal procedures are associated to a laparotomy before the anastomosis fashioning. This lengthens the procedure, obliging the surgeon to leave his robotic console. We recently adopted a new anastomotic method with an intracorporeal insertion of the anvil at the beginning of the operation, thus postponing the abdominal incision.

We recently adopted a new anastomotic method with an intracorporeal insertion of the anvil at the beginning of the operation, thus postponing the abdominal incision. In the present video, we illustrate the technical steps for fashioning the colorectal anastomosis, including the assessment of colon vascularization with the ICG.
The video demonstrates the technical steps for creating a colorectal anastomosis, including the assessment of colon vascularization using indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence imaging.

The patient is positioned supine with legs apart in a Trendelenburg position. The robotic surgical system is docked over the patient's left hip. Three robotic trocars are visible: one placed in the umbilical region, another in the epigastrium, and the third in the left flank.

The patient is positioned supine with legs apart in a 25-degree Trendelenburg position. The robotic SI system is docked over the patient's left hip. Three robotic trocars are inserted: one in the umbilical region, one in the epigastrium, and the third in the left flank.
After dividing the mesentery, the surgeon uses a fluorescent dye to assess perfusion. A colotomy is performed using monopolar scissors, cutting through the colon tissue.
After dividing the mesentery, the surgeon uses a fur fly to assess perfusion. A colotomy is performed using a monopolar old scissor.
The surgeon inserts the handwheel, which has a V-lock attached to its spike, into the colonic lumen. The colotum is then closed using a running V-lock trio suture.
The surgeon inserts the handwheel with a V-lock attached to its spike into the colon lumen. The colotum is then closed using a running V-lock trio suture.
The robotic stapler is used to transect the colon just proximal to the colotomy site, while the anvil anchoring the V-lock remains intact.
The robotic stapler is used to transect the colon just proximal to the colotomy site, while the anvil anchoring the V-lock remains intact. The transection of the colon is completed.
The surgeon completes the transection of the colon using a hot scissor to cut the staple line between the spike and the suture.
The surgeon uses a hot scissor to cut the staple line between the spike and the suture. The spike is gently removed from the colon and detached from the hand-wheel.
The surgeon gently removes the spike from the colon and detaches it from the hand-wheel. A hand-sewn anastomosis is then fashioned between the two ends of the colon.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn anastomosis, using the forefly to assess perfusion at the anastomotic site. A leak test is conducted, and the colorectal anastomosis is evaluated with a flexible endoscope.
The surgeon uses the forefly device to assess the perfusion of the anastomosis, followed by a higher leak test to ensure the integrity of the surgical connection.
The surgeon assesses the colorectal anastomosis using a flexible endoscope, visually inspecting the integrity and alignment of the anastomotic site.

Twenty patients underwent this robotic surgical technique. Only in two cases we observed a disposition of the anvil inside the lumen of the colon. No anastomotic complication and no wound infection occurred in the post-op course.

Twenty patients underwent this robotic surgical technique. Only in two cases we observed a disposition of the anvil inside the lumen of the colon. No anastomotic complication and no wound infection occurred in the post-op course.
In conclusion, this technique seems to provide potential advantage in obese patients. It requires less mobilization and extraction of the colon and a more flexible extraction site.
